items.q: What are the four pillars of food security?
items.correct: Availability (enough food exists), access (people can obtain it), utilisation (nutritional quality and absorption) and stability (consistent over time) — defined by the FAO.
items.misconception: Food security just means producing enough food.
items.random_wrong: Food security means storing food for emergencies.
items.why: Most modern famines occur where food is available but inaccessible (Amartya Sen's entitlement theory). Production alone doesn't deliver security — distribution, affordability and nutrition all matter.

items.correct: The sequence of changes in a community of organisms over time, from pioneer species colonising bare ground to a stable climax community matched to local climate.
items.misconception: Succession is just one species replacing another randomly.
items.random_wrong: Succession means a forest growing taller each year.
items.why: Succession is directional and predictable, not random. Each stage modifies conditions making the next stage possible — students miss the role of organisms in changing their own environment.

items.q: What is the Walker circulation and how does ENSO disrupt it?
items.correct: Walker circulation is the east-west tropical Pacific atmospheric loop: trade winds push warm water west, air rises in Indonesia, sinks over Peru. El Nino weakens it, La Nina strengthens it.
items.misconception: El Nino is a current that flows from Peru to Australia.
items.random_wrong: Walker circulation is the rotation of the Earth around the sun.
items.why: Students treat El Nino as a place or current; it's an oscillating state of a coupled ocean-atmosphere system. Walker circulation is the normal state — ENSO refers to its periodic flipping between phases.
It's Mumbai. “Dhaka” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Urban heat island raises temperatures by approximately?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1-3 degrees. “0.1 degree” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly how many people live in Dharavi (thousand)?
items.why: Exact answer: 700 thousand. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
